data.name: Mouhib Chamakh
data.description: “I dream of wearing the shirt of a major club and then taking part in the World Cup with my national team,” Chamakh said in 2019, when he was a teenager playing in Tunisia’s second tier. After a sensational season in goal for the first-time top-flight champions, Club Africain, he has got his wish – and could even replace Aymen Dahmen as the No 1 this summer. Chamakh impressed in his first cap against Canada in March, keeping a clean sheet in Toronto, and is one of a number of fresh faces in Sabri Lamouchi’s squad. “We must be brave enough to open the door and give our young players a chance,” Lamouchi has said. Having developed at Tunisian sides Médenine and Métlaoui, the 24-year-old is on the verge of fulfilling his childhood dream, with reports suggesting he could get the nod to start in North America.

data.name: Ali Abdi
data.description: A true success story. Few people believed Abdi could make it in Europe when he left Club Africain for Paris FC in 2019, at the age of 26. But, through spells in Ligue 2 with Paris FC and Caen, followed by a move to Ligue 1 with Nice, Abdi has proven himself to be one of those rare left-backs capable of changing a match on his own. He has also succeeded one of Tunisia’s legendary defenders, Ali Maâloul, in the national side. Before reaching this level, Abdi endured many hardships. He struggled with injuries, including pubalgia, which limited his chances at Espérance before he joined their cross-city rivals. Named Tunisia’s player of the year in 2024 and 2025, Abdi continues to give everything for the national team shirt. Tunisia’s first-choice player in his position remains a key figure in Sabri Lamouchi’s setup thanks to his defensive nous and attacking contributions.

data.name: Montassar Talbi
data.description: Born in Paris, the centre-back Talbi played youth football for Paris FC before his family returned to Tunisia when he was 12. He continued his development at Espérance de Tunis, before making the leap to join the Turkish side Caykur Rizespor at the age of 20, moving on to Rubin Kazan and then the French side Lorient. Talbi made his first appearance for the national team at 22 and has since become an undisputed starter in Sabri Lamouchi’s well-drilled defence. Named Tunisia’s player of the year in 2023, Talbi is a true warrior and one of the names almost certain to feature in the starting XI at this year’s World Cup.

data.name: Omar Rekik
data.description: Rekik has made the final 26-man squad after a rocky road with the national team. Called up in 2021 by the then coach, Mondher Kebaier, the 24-year-old defender had only played a few minutes for Tunisia, and even distanced himself from the national team setup. It was only after Sabri Lamouchi took charge that Rekik agreed to return. His older brother, Karim, has four caps for the Netherlands and Omar earned youth caps with the Oranje. The siblings moved together from Feyenoord to Manchester City, PSV, Marseille and Hertha Berlin, before Omar joined Arsenal in 2021 after Karim moved to Sevilla. Rekik did not play for the Gunners and was loaned out five times before joining the Slovenian side Maribor last summer – but he could very well feature at this summer’s tournament.

data.name: Adem Arous
data.description: A new discovery for many. Born in Saudi Arabia, Arous first broke through at Stade Tunisien. The centre-back then moved to Kasimpasa, a Turkish club previously home to several Tunisian internationals such as Syam Ben Youssef, Yassine Meriah, Oussama Haddadi and Mortadha Ben Ouanes, who still plays there today. His adaptation to this new environment in Istanbul was smooth, and earned him a place in the Tunisia squad for the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations. Arous did not get off the bench during the Eagles of Carthage’s time in Morocco, but he earned his first cap against Haiti in March. Still only 21, the future belongs to him and his profile could rise this summer, with Galatasaray and Monaco among sides showing an interest in signing him.

data.name: Dylan Bronn
data.description: One of only two players in the squad set to participate in their third World Cup, Bronn is one of the veterans of this Tunisia team. The 30-year-old centre-back, who has barely played for his club in Switzerland since January, was not guaranteed a place in the final squad. Asked about Bronn’s limited playing time, the Tunisia head coach, Sabri Lamouchi, explained: “Dylan has an important influence within the group. I personally asked his coach at Servette not to play him after his return from injury.” First capped in 2017, Bronn – who was born in Cannes to a German father and a Tunisian mother – played at right-back against England at the 2018 World Cup, and also scored a header in the 5-2 defeat by Belgium.

data.name: Elias Achouri
data.description: He rarely speaks to the media, but on the pitch Achouri is one of Tunisia’s most influential attacking players. Supporters regard the hard-working winger as being capable of changing a match single-handed. Achouri arrives at the World Cup in peak form after an impressive campaign with Copenhagen, scoring five goals and notching seven assists in all competitions. At Afcon 2025, he scored twice in the 3-1 win over Uganda, but missed a penalty in the shootout loss to Mali. Born on the French island of Réunion to a Tunisian father and an Algerian mother, Achouri’s playing career has taken him to France, Portugal and now Denmark. He appears almost certain to start this summer and could be the team’s key creative spark.

data.name: Elias Saad
data.description: Very few players successfully make the transition from futsal to professional football, but Saad is one of them. Born in Hamburg, he joined the city’s futsal team in 2019 and became the youngest player to make an appearance for Germany’s national futsal side. He was also playing in local amateur 11-a-side leagues and training for a career in sales when St Pauli came calling. The winger made an immediate impact, scoring in a derby defeat to Hamburg and impressing his coach, Fabian Hürzeler, who said: “Hard work is what he demands, and he’ll be rewarded.” Now 26, Saad is preparing to experience his first World Cup. Having scored three goals in two qualifying matches, he is among the players most eagerly awaited by fans in the United States, Mexico, and Canada this summer.

data.name: Hazem Mastouri
data.description: Mastouri’s journey to the top has been truly remarkable. At the age of 24, the striker was still playing in Tunisia’s second division. Step by step, he climbed the ladder, delivering goals for the Iraqi side Al-Najaf before joining Monastir. Mastouri, who stands 1.91m (6ft 3in) tall, scored 16 goals as his team finished second in the Tunisian top flight, earning him a move to the Russian Premier League. Last November, he scored Tunisia’s goal in a 1-1 friendly draw with Brazil in Lille. “Playing for the national team and scoring against Brazil feels like a dream,” the 28-year-old said afterwards. “I tell every young player to believe in their dreams, stay strong, and never give up.”

data.name: Hannibal Mejbri
data.description: Without a doubt, the biggest star of the Tunisia team. In 2021, the national football federation organised a special press conference to officially present Mejbri, in a first for a dual-nationality player choosing Tunisia. The young midfielder was emerging at Manchester United and had been capped by France at youth level, but said: “My heart chose Tunisia. You do not choose a national team by default or keep a country waiting.” Not only is Mejbri a star on the pitch, but he also speaks with remarkable maturity, something that greatly appeals to Tunisian fans. After Tunisia’s surprise elimination against Mali at the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations, he criticised the state of football development in Tunisia. “We dream a lot, but we do not work enough. We do not educate ourselves. We must ask ourselves serious questions because we have fallen far behind.” Now at Burnley, the playmaker who earned comparisons with David Beckham while at United’s academy always stood out, as one of them said: “He had a unique style, with big blond hair. Where you could find a pitch and a ball, you would find Hannibal.”

data.name: Ismaël Gharbi
data.description: Born in Paris, Gharbi played at youth level for France and Spain, before declaring for Tunisia in August 2025 after years of negotiations. After spending time in Paris FC’s youth setup, the versatile midfielder moved to Paris Saint-Germain’s academy in 2016 and broke into first-team contention five years later. The former PSG player Mathieu Bodmer has credited Gharbi with having “cold blood … In the penalty area, time stops for him.” His club career has stalled, though. After moving to Braga on a five-year deal, Gharbi spent last season at Augsburg, making just six Bundesliga appearances. Asked about his call-up for the March training camp despite his limited playing time at club level, Sabri Lamouchi explained: “He is not playing because of choices made at his club. But for me, he can bring a tremendous amount to this national team.”

data.name: Mortadha Ben Ouanes
data.description: In Sabri Lamouchi’s squad, Ben Ouanes is listed as a midfielder, but this versatile player can also operate as a left-back or left-winger. He has played in all three positions in Turkey since joining Kasimpasa in 2021; hos clubmate Adem Arous, 10 years his junior, is also in the Tunisia squad for this summer. Regularly called up since 2019, Ben Ouanes has only been used sparingly by the national team. He has made 17 appearances in total at the time of writing, with one particularly memorable match: Tunisia’s 5-1 friendly defeat against Brazil in 2022, where he started as a left-back. Tunisian supporters continue to share the same opinion: Ben Ouanes is a player whose full potential has never truly been harnessed by the national team.

data.name: Rani Khedira
data.description: At the age of 32, Rani Khedira finally agreed to represent Tunisia, switching allegiance from Germany just three months before the World Cup finals. Capped only at youth level for his country of birth, Khedira – younger brother of the World Cup winner Sami – had declined previous offers to represent the Eagles of Carthage. Sources close to the Tunisian federation have claimed that Khedira justified his refusal by saying he was not ready and spoke only German. This year, he finally accepted the call-up after talks with Sabri Lamouchi, who may even start him in a tough group. Many fans are sceptical over his change of heart, and believe Khedira is simply adding another line to his CV: playing in a World Cup. Tunisian supporters have not forgotten similar cases in the past, such as David Jemmali in 2006 or Yohann Benalouane in 2018, but Lamouchi appears convinced Khedira will improve his team.

data.name: Khalil Ayari
data.description: Fans believe Ayari is one of the players destined to shape the future of the national team. Last summer, he earned the opportunity to join the European champions, Paris Saint-Germain, at the age of 21. Tunisians know Ayari is talented, but they are equally aware he now has the perfect environment to evolve, improve, and reach another level. He received an excellent football upbringing at one of Tunisia’s biggest clubs, Stade Tunisien, before a successful trial took him to Paris. Supporters are also pleased because Ayari, with the support of his club, chose a move to Europe despite receiving enormous financial offers from Gulf-based clubs. Expectations surrounding the young winger are now extremely high, despite winning a solitary cap at the time of writing. It is up to him to seize this rare opportunity and prove himself on the biggest stage.

data.name: Hadj Mahmoud
data.description: Approaching the end of his contract with the Swiss side Lugano, the defensive midfielder is taking his first real steps with the Tunisia team. After starting against Mali during the recent Africa Cup of Nations in Morocco, Mahmoud earned Sabri Lamouchi’s trust with two appearances in the March friendlies against Haiti and Canada, but still faces a fight to start in a highly competitive midfield unit this summer. Mahmoud joined Étoile du Sahel in his home city of Sousse at the age of seven, before moving to Lugano in 2021 and helping them win the Swiss Cup. Having been linked with Union Berlin and Fenerbahce in the past, this World Cup could also serve as the perfect platform for Mahmoud to find a new club.

data.name: Aymen Dahmen
data.description: Outstanding during the 2022 World Cup in Qatar, the experienced goalkeeper is preparing to take part in his second World Cup finals. An undisputed starter for the Eagles of Carthage throughout the qualifiers – where he didn’t concede a goal – and during the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations, Dahmen missed the friendly matches against Haiti and Canada in March, with Sabri Lamouchi handing a start to each of his two other squad keepers. Dahmen plays for his hometown club, CS Sfaxien, who have turned down lucrative offers to keep him as their No 1. It remains to be seen whether Dahmen will wear that shirt for Tunisia’s opening match against Sweden in Monterrey.

data.name: Ellyes Skhiri
data.description: Elegant in his style of play, physically strong, and incredibly generous on the pitch, Skhiri perfectly embodies the description often associated with him: the quiet strength of Tunisia’s midfield. Like Dylan Bronn, the midfielder is preparing to take part in his third World Cup – but this time, he will be captain after the previous incumbent, Ferjani Sassi, was omitted from the squad. Skhiri featured for Tunisia Under-23s in 2014 but did not earn his first senior cap until 2018. “I decided to take my time,” he explained after choosing to represent Tunisia. “I am ready to balance both club and national team commitments. Until now, I prioritised my club [Montpellier at the time] because I needed to grow and establish myself.” Named the country’s player of the year in 2021 while at Cologne, Skhiri joined Eintracht Frankfurt in 2023 and has now made 200 Bundesliga appearances.

data.name: Rayan Elloumi
data.description: The youngest player in Tunisia’s squad, the 18-year-old forward has played for two different nations in 2026. Born in Edmonton, Elloumi chose to represent Tunisia at under-23 level last October, but featured for Canada in a B-level friendly against Guatemala in January, and informed the Tunisian football federation that he was considering switching his allegiance to the World Cup co-hosts. The response was immediate, with Lamouchi calling him up straight away for a March training camp. Elloumi played for 60 minutes against Haiti, then 30 minutes against Canada, before taking his place in Tunisia’s World Cup squad. Plucked from the local youth leagues by the Vancouver Whitecaps, he became their first homegrown player to score on his home MLS debut, netting in a 7-0 rout of Philadelphia Union.

data.name: Firas Chaouat
data.description: Top scorer of the Tunisian 2024-2025 season with Étoile du Sahel (17 goals) and again in 2025-2026 with Club Africain (14 goals), Chaouat is a true attacking powerhouse capable of troubling even the toughest defence. “He can score against any team,” says his Club Africain coach, Faouzi Benzarti. Shy in front of microphones but ruthless in front of goal, Chaouat is a natural-born scorer who played a crucial role in Club Africain’s league title triumph this season. The 30-year-old striker has finally reached the peak of his abilities – he earned more caps in 2025 than in the previous six years put together. Chaouat could be a key offensive weapon for Sabri Lamouchi as he looks to create scoring opportunities against Sweden, Japan and the Netherlands.

data.name: Yan Valery
data.description: When Valery broke into the Southampton first-team setup under Mark Hughes, he was already capped at youth level by France. The right-back first agreed to represent Tunisia in 2019 after a meeting with Alain Giresse, the then national-team coach. Valery was not called up until 2022, when he featured in a pre-World Cup friendly against Brazil but did not make the squad in Qatar. He did, however, play in every game as Tunisia reached the last 16 at the recent Africa Cup of Nations, and chose to leave struggling Sheffield Wednesday for a loan spell in the Swiss league with Young Boys in January. The gamble to try to secure his place in Sabri Lamouchi’s 26-man squad has paid off. Valery made the cut and is even considered Tunisia’s first-choice right-back heading into the World Cup. He would be the first Wednesday player to feature at a World Cup since John Sheridan played for the Republic of Ireland in 1994.

data.name: Mohamed Amine Ben Hamida
data.description: Primarily a left-back who can also operate in central defence, Ben Hmida has been described as “every coach’s dream” by Sabri Lamouchi, who values the 30-year-old as a “versatile, serious, and hard working” defender. Ben Hmida has limited experience in major international tournaments for Tunisia. He was an undisputed starter at the 2021 Arab Cup in Qatar, where Tunisia reached the final, but only played one minute across three matches in the 2025 edition, in which the team went out in the group stage. Ben Hmida describes himself as a “master of mental preparation” on social media, and has won five Tunisian titles in seven years at Espérance de Tunis.

data.name: Sabri Ben Hessen
data.description: A goalkeeper who had represented Tunisia at every youth level – including at the 2013 Under-17 World Cup – finally earned his first senior cap in March’s friendly against Haiti, where he kept a clean sheet in a 1-0 win. The wait was long and he had turned 29 by the time it happened, but Ben Hessen’s hard work and perseverance eventually paid off. Like Dahmen, he was born in Sfax and developed at his hometown club, where competition for his position was fierce. Ben Hassen moved to Étoile Sportive du Sahel last summer, where he has truly flourished. Delighted to be part of the final squad, he has said: “I fought to be here, I worked in silence, I stayed patient, and my dream is finally coming true.”

data.name: Moutaz Neffati
data.description: One of many young talents included in the squad, the right-back Neffati carries the hopes of many Tunisia supporters, but must first establish himself within the national team setup before aiming for a starting role. Born in Norrköping, Neffati represented Sweden at youth level before choosing to play for Tunisia last year. After a short loan in Iceland with KR, Neffati has become a regular with his hometown club and contributes goals and assists in the Swedish second tier. He now needs to confirm his potential with the national team. “Always proud and honoured to represent my country,” Neffati posted on Instagram after his Tunisia debut. “Inshallah, we have more to achieve.”

data.name: Raed Chikhaoui
data.description: The surprise inclusion in Sabri Lamouchi’s squad. Chikhaoui will turn 22 just before this summer’s World Cup kicks off, and had never been called up to the national team before Lamouchi’s appointment as Tunisia head coach in January. Chikhaoui moved to the coastal city of Monastir from Hammam-Sousse in 2024, and has become an undisputed starter in defence, helping USM finish fifth in the league last season. Primarily a left-sided centre-back, Chikhaoui is nicknamed the Watchtower due to his 1.90m (6ft 3in) height and versatility. His selection represents a major opportunity that this talented young defender will now have to seize, and could earn him a move to Europe with several clubs monitoring his development.

data.name: Anis Ben Slimane
data.description: A midfield talent unlike any other in the squad, there is a widespread feeling that Ben Slimane has never truly been used to his full potential with the national team. Having represented Denmark at under-19 level before playing for Tunisia at the 2022 World Cup, the Copenhagen-born playmaker is still waiting for that one match to fully showcase his immense talent. Absent from the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations squad, he is coming off an excellent season with Norwich despite injury setbacks: 26 matches, five goals, and six assists. The Canaries decided to make his loan move from Sheffield United permanent in January. Back in the fold internationally, Ben Slimane has more than enough in his locker to earn a place in the squad.

data.name: Sebastian Tounekti
data.description: Having performed exceptionally for Celtic in his first season in Scotland, Tounekti is another player who is expected to shape the future of the Tunisia team. The left-winger was born in Tromsø and chose to represent the Eagles of Carthage after turning out for Norway at a number of youth levels. Following his debut in 2021, Tounekti had to wait four years to earn another call-up. Despite that, he appears to have a very strong chance of starting in Tunisia’s attacking line at his first World Cup, having scored the winner against Haiti in a Toronto friendly in March. Tounekti’s blistering pace is expected to cause serious problems for opposing defenders. Expectations are high, and he possesses all the talent necessary to deliver and confirm his potential.
